overview.title: World War II Overview
overview.description: Select a cluster to explore a key dimension of World War II, then choose a node to examine a more focused idea.

sections.title: Why this topic matters
sections.text: World War II is a rich historical topic because it can be understood through causes, events, experiences, change, significance and debate. A learning map helps break the topic into connected clusters without losing the larger picture.

sections.title: Guiding question
sections.quote: How do causes, experiences, turning points and legacies help us understand World War II and its significance for Australia?

clusters.title: Causes of the Second World War
clusters.description: This cluster explores the political, economic and ideological conditions that contributed to the outbreak of war.

nodes.title: Treaty of Versailles
nodes.subtitle: aftermath of World War I
nodes.description: The Treaty of Versailles imposed harsh penalties on Germany, contributing to resentment, instability and later political extremism.
nodes.significance: This node helps students connect the end of one war to the causes of another, showing how unresolved tensions can shape future conflict.

content.caption: Placeholder image for the Treaty of Versailles.

content.text: The peace settlement after World War I became part of the story of how a later conflict emerged.

items.title: Treaty signed
items.text: Germany accepted harsh peace terms after World War I.

items.label: 1920s
items.title: Resentment grows
items.text: Economic hardship and political instability deepened anger in Germany.

items.label: 1930s
items.title: Revisionism rises
items.text: Nazi policy drew on widespread opposition to the treaty.

nodes.title: Rise of dictators
nodes.subtitle: Germany, Italy and Japan
nodes.description: Authoritarian leaders promoted nationalism, militarism and expansion, increasing international tension.
nodes.significance: This highlights how leadership, ideology and political systems influenced the path to war.

content.caption: Placeholder image for authoritarian leadership and propaganda.

items.title: Mussolini
items.text: Mussolini came to power in Italy.

items.label: 1930s
items.title: Expansionism
items.text: Aggressive nationalist policies intensified across Europe and Asia.

nodes.title: Failure of collective security
nodes.subtitle: League of Nations and weak responses
nodes.description: International efforts to prevent aggression were limited, allowing expansionist states to act with fewer consequences.
nodes.significance: This helps students examine why international systems sometimes fail to prevent conflict.

nodes.title: Appeasement
nodes.subtitle: responses of Britain and France
nodes.description: Some governments tried to avoid war by making concessions, a policy that remains debated by historians.
nodes.significance: This node opens up interpretation and debate, not just factual recall.

content.caption: Placeholder image for appeasement and diplomatic negotiation.

content.text: Appeasement is often discussed not just as a decision, but as a historical debate.

items.title: Munich
items.text: European leaders accepted German demands over Czechoslovakia.

items.title: Limits exposed
items.text: Appeasement ended when Germany invaded Poland.

clusters.title: Australian Experiences of World War II
clusters.description: This cluster examines how Australians experienced the war through military service, fear of invasion, labour changes and social disruption.

nodes.title: Service and sacrifice
nodes.subtitle: soldiers, nurses and prisoners of war
nodes.description: Australians served in different theatres of war, facing danger, hardship and loss.
nodes.significance: This helps students move beyond broad national narratives to the lived experiences of individuals and groups.

nodes.title: The war in the Pacific
nodes.subtitle: regional threat and strategy
nodes.description: The Pacific War brought the conflict closer to Australia and reshaped strategic priorities.
nodes.significance: This is central to understanding why World War II had a distinct meaning for Australia.

content.caption: Placeholder image for the Pacific theatre.

items.title: Pearl Harbor
items.text: Japan attacked Pearl Harbor and expanded the war in the Pacific.

items.title: Darwin bombed
items.text: Australian vulnerability became more immediate and visible.

items.title: Kokoda
items.text: Campaigns in Papua became central to Australian memory.

nodes.title: Women’s roles
nodes.subtitle: expanded responsibilities
nodes.description: Women took on new wartime roles in services, industry and community life, changing expectations and opportunities.
nodes.significance: This supports discussion of both wartime change and the limits of that change.

nodes.title: Fear and uncertainty
nodes.subtitle: air raids and invasion anxiety
nodes.description: Events such as the bombing of Darwin heightened fear and changed how Australians understood their security.
nodes.significance: This helps students connect national identity to vulnerability, geography and wartime experience.

clusters.title: Turning Points of the Second World War
clusters.description: This cluster focuses on key events that shifted momentum, strategy or public understanding during the war.

nodes.title: Fall of France
nodes.description: Germany’s rapid victory in Western Europe shocked the world and reshaped Allied strategy.
nodes.significance: This shows how early Axis successes transformed expectations about the scale and nature of the war.

nodes.title: Pearl Harbor
nodes.description: Japan’s attack on Pearl Harbor brought the United States directly into the war.
nodes.significance: This node matters because it changed the scale, alliances and resources involved in the conflict.

nodes.title: Battle of Stalingrad
nodes.description: A major turning point on the Eastern Front, weakening German momentum and boosting Soviet advance.
nodes.significance: This helps students see that turning points were global, not limited to a single front.

nodes.title: D-Day
nodes.description: The Allied invasion of Normandy marked a decisive step in liberating Western Europe.
nodes.significance: This node is often central in public memory, making it useful for comparing historical significance and commemoration.

items.title: France falls
items.text: Axis power expanded quickly in Western Europe.

items.title: US enters war
items.text: Pearl Harbor widened the scale of the conflict.

items.title: Stalingrad
items.text: German momentum weakened on the Eastern Front.

items.title: D-Day
items.text: Allied forces opened a major Western front.

clusters.title: Home Front: Continuity and Change
clusters.description: This cluster explores what changed and what stayed the same in Australian society during the war years.

nodes.title: Rationing and restrictions
nodes.subtitle: daily life under pressure
nodes.description: Government controls affected food, clothing, fuel and ordinary routines across society.
nodes.significance: This makes the war visible in everyday life, not just in battles and leaders.

nodes.title: Propaganda and censorship
nodes.subtitle: information and persuasion
nodes.description: Governments used media to influence morale, behaviour and public support for the war effort.
nodes.significance: This supports discussion of power, persuasion and the shaping of public opinion.

nodes.title: Work and industry
nodes.subtitle: expanded production
nodes.description: The demands of total war changed employment, manufacturing and expectations of labour.
nodes.significance: This helps students connect wartime demands to broader economic and social change.

nodes.title: Continuity and inequality
nodes.subtitle: limits of change
nodes.description: Not all wartime changes were equal or permanent; some patterns of social hierarchy and exclusion remained.
nodes.significance: This is useful for historical thinking because it resists oversimplified 'war changed everything' narratives.

clusters.title: Post-war Significance for Australia
clusters.description: This cluster considers how the war reshaped Australia politically, socially and internationally.

nodes.title: Foreign policy shift
nodes.subtitle: from Britain to the United States
nodes.description: The war contributed to a major change in Australia’s international relationships and strategic outlook.
nodes.significance: This is central to understanding long-term national significance rather than only wartime events.

nodes.title: National identity
nodes.subtitle: confidence and independence
nodes.description: The war influenced how Australians saw their place in the region and in the world.
nodes.significance: This helps students explore how war can shape identity, memory and political culture.

nodes.title: Migration and reconstruction
nodes.subtitle: post-war rebuilding
nodes.description: Post-war policies changed population, infrastructure and the long-term shape of Australian society.
nodes.significance: This links wartime experience to later change, showing that consequences extend beyond 1945.

nodes.title: Human rights and global institutions
nodes.subtitle: wider legacies
nodes.description: The war contributed to new international efforts around peace, law and human rights.
nodes.significance: This expands the frame from national significance to global consequence.

clusters.title: Commemoration and Debate
clusters.description: This cluster explores how World War II has been remembered, interpreted and debated over time.

nodes.title: Memorials and ceremonies
nodes.subtitle: public remembrance
nodes.description: Commemoration practices help shape how communities remember sacrifice, service and loss.
nodes.significance: This allows students to see remembrance as an active process, not just a neutral reflection of the past.

nodes.title: Whose stories are told?
nodes.subtitle: perspective and inclusion
nodes.description: Historical memory can privilege some voices while overlooking others, including civilians, women and minority groups.
nodes.significance: This supports perspective-taking and critical discussion about representation.

nodes.title: Debates about significance
nodes.subtitle: history and interpretation
nodes.description: Historians and the public may disagree about which events, decisions or experiences mattered most.
nodes.significance: This helps students understand that history involves interpretation as well as evidence.

nodes.title: History and national identity
nodes.subtitle: uses of the past
nodes.description: Interpretations of World War II can influence ideas about citizenship, patriotism and Australia’s place in the world.
nodes.significance: This makes the topic relevant to contemporary civic and cultural life.
